does: This paper documents the architectural design of OWL, a browser architecture that decouples the rendering engine from traditional browser components to enable ChatGPT integration. The system achieves fast startup times and supports rich UI interactions by restructuring how Chromium interacts with application logic. OWL enables agentic browsing capabilities, allowing AI models to autonomously interact with web pages through a redesigned interface layer that exposes browser functionality to language models in a way that facilitates both human and AI-driven navigation tasks.
connects: This work builds on decades of browser architecture evolution, responding to constraints of monolithic designs like those in Chromium. It connects to recent research on AI agents and tool use, where language models learn to interact with external systems through structured APIs. The architecture also draws from work on decoupled system design and microservice patterns, applying these principles to browser technology. It extends prior work on integrating AI assistants into productivity tools, taking lessons from copilot systems and conversational interfaces while addressing the specific challenges of exposing web automation to language models.
enables: OWL opens pathways for more sophisticated AI-assisted web interaction, where agents can accomplish multi-step browsing tasks autonomously or collaboratively with users. The decoupled architecture enables experimentation with alternative UI paradigms beyond traditional graphical interfaces, potentially supporting voice, gesture, or other modalities. It facilitates better integration of language models into everyday web tasks—research, shopping, form-filling, and information gathering. The approach also positions future work on learning user preferences, adapting agent behavior based on context, and building more specialized versions for particular domains or use cases.
